53. Hammer Them - Long Form Explainer 1-31-25.mp4 All right, ladies and gentlemen, we are updating the hammer-them strategy to include long-form content. Long-form content has really increased in consumption. There's this great book. I reference it throughout my content. Rather frequently. It's called Pendulum. The concept is simple. At different points in human history, different things are widely accepted. And it's periods of time where those same things that were historically widely accepted all of a sudden are widely rejected. And it swings back and forth. Rarely does it just stay one-sided. And for a long duration of time, at least for these most recent years, short-form content has been what's consumed the most. And to be very clear with you, it still is consumed at the highest rate comparatively to long-form content. But what's happened most recently is that long-form content has worked its way into the buying process for what we've seen consistently across every one of our deals. More than half of the sales that are occurring are consistently saying, and this is both from obviously organic, but mainly paid advertising. This was really interesting when we broke it down and looked at the analytics. Here's what the data pointed to in terms of the conclusions that we were able to pull from it after reflecting on it. Long-form content. Regardless, of us as the advertisers working it in to the advertising process or the sales people working it into the sales process was still being consumed and not just consumed. Binged, okay? That's the key word I want to make clear. Binged at a very high rate. So what we chose to do, rather than just letting it be consumed on its own, we chose to intentionally weave it into the sales process and to the advertising. Process as well. There's two strategies that we use to do this currently: number one, which you can watch in a separate video training from me, is the Venus Flytrap 2. 0. That's right. We revamped my original content advertising strategy from over a decade ago and chose to relaunch it again. We typically split, test, and compare it against driving traffic straight to a conversion mechanism. I won't sit here and make this entire video about the Venus Flytrap 2. 0, but I will make this clear. The Venus Flytrap 2. 0 is at the beginning of the process before the direct response. Whereas everything that happens with the hammer them sequence, both short and long form, happens after. The direct response so when we use the hammer them, and we use it in both examples to be clear, we are going to incorporate it. After the person takes an action, regardless, we're adding in the hammer them sequence. Okay? But... when we run direct response on its own, which let me just give you some examples: you tell people, 'Hey, go opt in for my webinar,' 'Hey, go and book a call with us,' 'Uh, lock in a time to speak with our team you know, whatever it is, fill out a lead form. Again, doesn't matter what the conversion mechanism is or what the direct response is. The point I'm trying to make is you can do both, and preferably you do both, where you lead with direct response, and you can also lead with a content sequence, and then direct response. But in either scenario, after the direct response occurs, and the person takes an action, we are going to hammer them, okay? Again, what I am here to talk to you about today is how we are going to incorporate the long form content into a. historically short form exclusive ad strategy. So let me refresh your memory just in case you are new to the hammer them and this coincidentally is one of the first videos you watch on this thing in depth. The hammer them strategy. is created out of pure rage and frustration. What was happening in 2021. and 2022 was salespeople would consistently bitch and whine about the comparison bias they were developing. from organic leads versus paid advertising leads. And just to be rather transparent, I mean, we lost a fair amount of deals as a result of having sales teams that we just could not successfully turn into cashiers. the deals that we made the most money on had the best sales teams that's really what it came down to so that became a really strong characteristic that we would consistently for in almost every single deal that we would do we would ensure that they had the best salespeople possible and if they didn't we wouldn't even take on the deal because of the risk The risk being we would lose the account. We would have refund potential. We would waste a lot of time. That perspective, in fairness to the salespeople, really blames the sales reps rather than accepting any responsibility that we can influence the dynamic in that relationship. And we... have a bunch of content ad strategies historically that we've run, ranging from the Forester, the Venus Flytrap 1. 0, the tornado, and then later on we developed this hammer them strategy and we made it a breakout strategy. It was originally a part of a much larger ad campaign called the tornado. And we broke it out on its own. We called it a supplement campaign. It had so much effectiveness at solving the problem that these salespeople were whining about, which was, 'Hey, these people aren't showing up sold already.' We want to have layup deals. You know, that was what I kept hearing these people say, in other words. The moral of the story is, again, the short-form hammer them strategy is very simple to understand. We lead with direct response and we tell the person, 'Hey, go opt in for our webinar.' Right after they opt in for the webinar, we are going to hammer them. We're going to take 30 to 50 different short-form pieces of content. and we are going to blitz that individual. We are going to have a frequency of a minimum of 15 to 20 within whatever the key time frame window is, as long as we have at least 100 people to be able to retarget. We can hammer them in these key windows. It's harder to do with call funnels if you're not running call funnels at scale. It's harder to do with lead forms, if you're not running lead forms at scale, you have to have a minimum of 100 people in these remarketing audiences. So for the people out there who are just getting started with advertising, meaning you're spending like tens of thousands of dollars in a month and you might not have, you know, let's just use the example of a call funnel. People are booking a call time. And then they have about 72 hours on average before the call time occurs. That specific 72-hour window is where we want to hammer them. That's ideally where we have a minimum of 100 people flowing through, which, to be fair, again, you have to. Be scaled up or in the process of scaling up aggressively to have that minimum of 100 people in that part of the process. I just want to reiterate as a pro tip. In the instance that you fit the bill currently of not having that minimum threshold met, you have to add a little supplement audience to beef up and meet that minimum requirement. A good example of some supplement audiences could be people who just had their call and are within your current average sales cycle timeline. So let's use the example that my average sales cycle is about two weeks on average. Well, everybody who had a call. Following the two weeks that they had that call time, we can put into an audience and we can add that in addition to the people who just booked their call. That ideally meets that minimum threshold if we and of course you'd exclude buyers from this if we did still not meet that minimum of 100 people. Maybe instead of two weeks, you did like four weeks, you know, again, whatever meets the minimum of 100 people. But you want to make sure that you always have a high quality person that you're going to spend this retargeting dollar on. That way, you're hammering the right people who it has the most impact on. And just to refresh your memory, again, whether it's a call funnel, whether it's an instant form, whether it's before a webinar happens. You're remarketing to these people with the short form hammer them sequence. You're following the four quadrants of content when you do the hammer them sequence. Those four quadrants being questions, second order questions, or second layer questions. Objections? and expectations are okay. and those four quadrants are what you're developing your 30 to 50 pieces of content around. and again, all these short-form pieces of content, when we originally created the Hammer Them strategy, are ideally optimized for vertical format, not like this. this would be a landscape format. we'd want to shoot them in a vertical format and optimize them for reels, which would ideally mean sub 90 seconds. okay. now I want to be clear: there is still extreme effectiveness in the short-form content and what the Hammer Them strategy solves, but it doesn't get people all the way there. it's what we've noticed. And that's why we've chosen to amplify it and step it up that much further by incorporating a long form section of the hammer them sequence as well. Now, the hammer them sequence, when you look at the short form, all of the logic of that strategy still applies. But the short form typically accomplishes a few key things. Short form, from what we're seeing, is solving, and these are the main things that it solves, okay? Number one, awareness. So let's use the example that you're taking content and you're putting it in front of people, and you're answering their questions that they're gonna have on the sales call, which is originally what you're doing with the short form content and the hammer them sequence. You're answering objections before they come up. You're setting expectations of how long it's going to take or how much time investment there is or whatever it is that the expectations are of your specific offer. You're answering common questions and the second layer questions that come up as a result of those original questions getting answered. And you're just putting them in front of people before they actually show up to whatever the conversion event is. So when I say it solves awareness, it's like. Thank you. Again, just to be extremely clear on what that means—like, by the time they show up to the sales call, they have all that data in their head and they've seen, like, 15 to 20 different answers and perspectives that you've shared with them before they get to the sales call. Before they go to the webinar, well, again, whatever the key event is that you're running in your direct response conversion mechanism, but it doesn't necessarily like. Make them show up sold. You know, it just makes them aware. And it advances the conversation. And this is the next thing that it does. When I say it advances the conversation. This is good. But there was one quote that really stuck out to me with one of the sales managers that we work with. And the quote was... what kills the sale or the sales call is the lead showing up for education. And I thought to myself, I was like, well, I mean, they have so much information that they're being exposed to. What does that mean? What does education mean? For what they're showing up and talking about. And for this particular sales manager, he was working with us on a few different offers. He was working with us on two different information product offers. He was also working with us on a high ticket service business, not an information product business, just a high ticket service business. And he was also working with us on a raising capital client. And I was like, is that perspective equally shared amongst all four of these different deal types? And he said, 'Oh, yeah, for sure.' It's very common. If people show up to the call seeking education rather than treating it like what it is, which is a sales call where the person is probably to close. There won't be a sale. At the very least, there'll be a second follow-up call and there'll be some consumption. He said, 'It really just comes down to what feels like—they're not consuming the information.' Like the people who do show up, sold the people who do show up, and treat it like a sales call, on average, from what they've seen and what they consistently report to us, are binging long-form content to make them feel safe and that was one of the most interesting perspectives I've heard in a long time. So, when we look at long-form and what it accomplishes, it accomplishes safety in decision making. And this was very interesting. So. There's a great book and we've referred to it time and time again. I love this book. I think every single marketer on earth should read this book many times over. And you will, if you ever have picked up this book, because that's how good it is. It's called Breakthrough Advertising by Eugene Schwartz. Probably one of the most expensive books you'll ever buy, but very worth it. And in one of the first chapters, he talks about this concept called market sophistication. And for all those unfamiliar, market sophistication means, well, when you very first pitch something and the person who you're pitching has never been pitched on it before, and they also ideally have the problem or want the solution or want whatever the outcome is or just want the product that you're selling. It's like lay down deals. You know, people can just hear what it is that you can do for them. And if they want it, there's a very high probability they'll show up with very little friction and buy whatever that is. That's stage one. Stage two is where, hey, you're cleaning house. OK, you're making so much money that the copy and paste people out there, the copy and paste business owners, who have. No original thought. They only take actions once something has been proven. The second wave entrepreneurs they start to take notice that you're being successful in getting some money and they say, 'I'd love some of that money too' and they start up a business competing with you. So now, what happens is in stage two, all you really have to do is to win. In the words of Eugene Schwartz, it's just outspend everybody else. You just got to reach the customers first and outspend everybody that's competing with you in order to continue to dominate the market. Stage three is where things get a little tricky. Same thing with stage four. And I see a lot of similarities between stage three and stage four in terms of how you have to treat it. So stage three is where the consumer has officially been pitched several times. They start to, rather than just immediately buy. They start to consider a little more, and some friction is officially added into the decision-making process because there's more than one option. Some paralysis can enter in, and you start to see sales cycles lengthen. You start to lose a little bit of money to competitors here and there. And on top of that, what generally starts to happen in phase three— not with everybody, but it starts to happen— is consumers start to get screwed over. They start to buy things. And as a result of them buying things and not seeing the results that were promised, skepticism is introduced into the market as well. So in stage three, what you have to start to do is you have to start to communicate your mechanism to result. Okay, you have to start to be very articulate about how you're going to get the person the result. This is where you start to see people in more that are in stage three, sophistication. Start to like really break down the quality of their product, the features of their product, the features and benefits of their services. They become more articulate rather than just making the claim, which no longer works. You have to be a lot more in depth about how you're going to go about getting the person the result. Okay. Now, stage four. Stage four is when people have been pitched dozens and dozens of times. This is like, as an example, computers. Any type of mainstream electronic. Cars are a great example of this. Plane tickets. Has the market been pitched? They've likely had several experiences with different service or product providers. Again, they've been screwed. They've potentially done good things as well with different people. Again, making a claim and just pitching something no longer works at all. You have to be able to not only market— in depth— explainers about how you're going to be able to get the person a result, but you also have to add a tremendous amount of additional follow-up material and what we call sales assets (trust assets). Okay, and I have in-depth videos explaining these trust assets and sales assets, and what you could do to create some of these things if you want to check that out here in the course library. You're more than welcome to. Thank you. So, without digressing too much, what I want to make clear is: And all four of these examples— with the sales manager that was giving me this feedback— when you really looked at it, a lot of these businesses were in stage four market sophistication or in very late stage, stage three market sophistication. and people were showing up treating the sales call like an education call rather than a sales call. Why, right? Why was that happening? They were getting blitzed with content, hammered with content, getting 15 to 20 different answers before the call would actually occur, increasing their awareness tremendously prior to the call or whatever the conversion event was. The salespeople were positively commenting. The differences that the short form content was making in warming leads up and at least advancing the conversation more, but here's what you got to understand when I say advancing the conversation. Okay. So, if this is the start, and over here represents where the close occurs along the way. There are some stages that you have to move a buyer through, so this start, generally, is awareness. OK, you have to make the person aware of whatever it is that you're selling and just introduce them to your process. OK, this is where you like capture a lead. Sometimes it's even higher up than that. Sometimes it's just like, OK, they see the first piece of content. Sometimes it's they just saw an ad and they didn't click on that ad. They didn't have time to do that yet. But they became aware of it, you know, and then, generally, from there, they start to go through and they take an action. Again, this might be opting in for something, booking a call, going to a webinar, you know. Again, this is just they've officially taken an action, but that by no means represents that they're sold. Allow me to pause real quick and just make this extremely clear. This is where a lot of you will become very confused. You think that because somebody went and booked a call or that somebody went and opted into a webinar or somebody went through your DM funnel strategy or whatever it is that you're running, they fill out your lead form and they expressed interest that they wanted to have a conversation with you. The illusion is that this person is sold already. That's not the case at all. Matter of fact, it's very far away from the truth and the perspective that you should maintain. You should treat it like, and this is where I kind of digress a little bit, but it's an important digression. We have this interest bar. Where I like to say they started curious. And then there's general interest. And then there's... highly interested. And then they're sold. Okay, so generally, when a lead just does something like book a call or fill out a lead form or opt into a webinar, or DM your business, or whatever it is. They're just curious. You know, if in a, in a. kind of like almost like a best case scenario. They're generally interested, especially in highly sophisticated markets. When you're in stage three or stage four market sophistication, the important thing to take note of is that they're probably not going to be coming in highly interested or sold unless they already have a tremendous amount of framing that another business has already invested into them having. Or they've just developed that perspective on their own, which, again, is fairly rare. You can, in some instances, and this is very important to note, in some instances, you can capitalize on more general interested and highly interested people when you specifically communicate to in-market demographics. And this is a big pro tip. If you do want to get people that are in those two higher levels of interest in market demographic communication is one of the most important things you can learn. We do have an entire video filmed in a setting like this. If you want to take a look for it, it's a white background with my Megalodon logo behind me and it says, 'In-Market Demographic Messaging Mastery.' You can watch that video to get an in-depth explainer on how to communicate to those types of demographics if you're not already competent on doing that. Back to my point. Most leads. Most leads. When they officially take the action. When they officially get to here. This is where the business gets the illusion. And why aren't people showing up like ready to buy? Okay. This is what has to happen next. And this kind of sounds a little bit ridiculous, but this is the perspective that you need to have. All of this— is the nurture steps. These following three steps, I want you to have three key points of nurture. This is very important to understand. We have short form nurture. We have the long form content nurture. And we have the sales people involved. These three different things, in combination, are what's critical in order to get to the point where you get the people— all three of these— collectively contribute to the probability of that close occurring or not. Okay, so with this sales manager that I was communicating with, I tried to lend him the same perspective that I had back in 2021 and 2022, where I was thinking, 'When you look out in the marketplace and you try to find anybody who has a very high success rate and is just crushing it with their funnels. It's really a combination of all three of these things. Their salespeople aren't being whiny.' About the fact that they're having to do some education, they adapt to either having a two-call-close process. or establishing communication prior to the call and doing some nurture via text. Sometimes these sales reps are even going as far as having like a pre-call today. Their call and you might call that a two-call close, but it's not. They'll just reach out to the lead and they'll successfully establish contact and they'll develop these resources. Okay. And I also want to be fair in saying, as like a side note to one of these, there's also something important to take note on, which is email does play a function in this. But email, depending on the open rates of your business, they're not going to impact everybody widely. The short form and the long form content, there is no opt out of that. You can get the 15 to 20 impression count, the frequency count. You can ensure that you have that before that conversion event occurs where they talk to the sales reps. Your sales reps can do things where the visibility is damn near 100% that the email and automated text just can't do. If I can get a salesperson to just do something as simple as, like, send a selfie video, if I can get the business owner to put together, like, a comprehensive document, like, essentially, just a references document. Where there's all of these resources, very well organized, easy to read through. Giving people answers with short-form content, long-form content, giving them references on like testimonials and people that they can call prior to the conversion event occurring. These things make a dramatic difference to the person showing up to the sales call and treating it like a sales call instead of an education call. They increase the probability of closes happening dramatically. But it's no longer a game of like, I hear some of these guys. There's actually a guy here in Miami who... He very commonly just says, like, 'Oh, we force every single person through long form content before we ever expose them to direct response.' And I just completely disagree with that because you lose such a tremendous amount of the audience that's never going to take that action prior to actually just going through your sales funnel first. You have to understand—like— you want to still allow people to go through the funnel and take the action, like allow them to book a call, allow them to enter the webinar, allow them to go through the instant form before they've ever seen any content. Allow them to still take those actions because you're going to capture far more people. Still going to make far more money than these little, small fries that force everybody through long-form content first, exclusively. Like you'll hear me when I talk about the Venus Vlad Trap 2. 0 in that training video. You'll hear me talk very clearly on the fact that we never run it. Force like we don't force people to have to go through that to get to the point where they see direct response— we'll still run direct response at the same exact time and we'll give people the option. We'll give people the choice. Do you want to watch a sequence of videos before you see direct response? But with a richer demographic, and that's the main thing I want you to understand— like in my business, we regularly sell to very affluent demographics. Rich people won't sit around and binge watch your content until they're already generally sold that you can help them. Once they get to the point where they develop a mindset that 'These people can actually help me,' then and only then will that demographic specifically choose to start binging your content. Before that, it's a risk of their time. And if you know any... rich person and you actually understand how they behave, rarely do they ever risk their time. They value time more than money in most instances. Yes, and they fucking love money. Okay. So you got to understand you don't force people through the content first. That's why I love the hammer them strategy. The hammer them strategy follows up with the content. So again, in this first phase, where you start your advertising process, you're getting people aware, you're getting people to take whatever the action is on your specific conversion mechanism, you then follow it up with those three key points: short form, long form, and you adapt your salespeople to modern sales tactics. You have to make your sales team aware. You, as a salesperson, play such a huge role in being able to reach people at a higher rate, just like what I can do with content. I can reach damn near 100% of the people who take whatever the action is with content before the call, before the webinar, and before the sales conversation. But you, as a salesperson, you can reach people that the marketing automation, like the emails and the automated texts, will only reach like a small fraction of the total people who took the action. So you being able to spend just that little bit of time as a sales rep. Messaging people via text, sending some manual emails, like you have a higher probability to land in the inbox comparatively to the emails that are being used to blast people. Scale consistently on behalf of the business. You have a higher probability, with your real phone number, to land in the person's SMS message inbox at a much higher rate than a fucking green text message from some automated phone number ever will. It's not like I'm trying to tell you, as a business owner or a marketer, that you need to neglect doing automated emails and SMS. I'm just trying to tell you it makes a huge fucking difference when the salespeople are willing to contribute to some of these manual, tedious things alongside of the long form and the short form content. Okay. Now let's get back to my point, now that you understand the problem. And what we're trying to solve by incorporating long form, okay? So the long form content has to be good. There's some long form. Kind of principles that I want to make clear to you that you have to follow if you really want to make this work. So first of all, I call this value density. Okay. The value density has to be extremely high. And I mean, extremely high, on these pieces of content you're going to use. You're going to try to cherry pick as much of your long form content as you can. But you also have to remember it's consumed differently than how it is on platforms like YouTube as an example. It's consumed differently. When you are injecting it into a person's news feed and it has that little sponsored word underneath your page, you're running the ads from. Okay, there's just a perceptual difference there because, again, they're scrolling mindlessly on platforms like Facebook, Instagram and TikTok, where you're ideally going to incorporate this long form content. You do this on LinkedIn as well. And also you could do this on YouTube, to be clear. With the value density, what I mean by that is these videos have to start off by essentially articulating like a very key problem. That your demographic faces. That your specific product or service solves. It cannot be like some frivolous bullshit. I'll give you an example with things that I can talk about myself inside of my business as a perfect example. In my business, people like you, you have a very key set of problems that make you more sold that I'm your guy and that I can help. An example of that is I can talk about the fact that you run call funnels and people show up too cold. I can talk about the fact that your sales team crushes with organic leads. And they suck ass every time you tried to run any kind of paid funnel. I could talk about the fact that you're stuck having to do webinars and challenge funnels, even though you hate the frequency that you have. Go live in your business and you'd much rather use the hands-off funnels, like lead forms and call funnels, and variations of those strategies, but you can't because your sales people can only close to people that are coming from webinars and challenge funnels. These are examples of things that I could lead a video with and then immediately, and I mean immediately, start talking about something that helps to solve that problem. A good example of this is almost every video I have on YouTube. What we do on my YouTube channel when we overhaul those videos, edit them, and make them useful for our long-form content. Hammer them with strategies. The thing you can take a lot of your YouTube videos, but your YouTube videos have to be chopped up in a specific way. Okay, you can't have the intros on them that are long and drawn out. Nobody gives a fuck about that. Okay? You have to cut out all the stuff where you tell people, like, 'Hey, like and subscribe and comment.' Cut that fucking shit out. Okay? People don't give a fuck. Fuck about that when you're incorporating this stuff into the long-form content. What do they care about? Value and the density of that value. The value can't be spread few and far between. It has to be like hit after hit after hit of things that make them sit there and say, 'And this is my guy right here Like this, this person has every single thing that I'm currently struggling with, and they're putting me on game, literally right here, right now, in this video. Imagine what the paid stuff that they have is like. You see what I mean. It can't just be as simple as, 'Hey everybody, what's going on?' My name's Jeremy Haines. I've got together a list of things that I think people who are selling information products and offer owners and really just anybody selling any kind of high ticket product or service would care about. I got some great things for you in this video today. Make sure you pay attention. Hey, by the way, do me a favor. If you like the video, share it. That is fucking whack. You will get ignored, okay? People won't watch. your fucking videos you will you will see such a steep drop off if you do anything like that like the videos have to start with you immediately talking about the problem they have in depth where it's like Here, imagine the video starts. Every business owner we work with selling high-ticket products or services hates the call funnel leads that come from paid ads. Their salespeople constantly complain about it because at the end of the day, the day they're just not warm enough is what the salespeople keep saying they're doing great with the organic leads, but the organic leads are consuming an unbound amount of content. What if you could replicate that with your paid advertising process? In my marketing agency, we created an ad strategy that we spent literally millions of dollars on, highly profitably. I'm going to break it down for you in this video for what can do to help solve that specific issue. Let's dive in and then I just immediately start fucking talking about it. There is no like bullshit fillers. Okay, none of that value density. Hit after hit after hit. It's like I would give you a good perspective of saying every like 45 seconds max. You better drop some fire shit that makes them sit there and say, like, 'This is good.' And when I say long form, one thing that I want to make clear, like these videos can be as low as about 10 minutes, as ridiculously short form as that might sound, all the way up to about two hours. Here's the thing. This is very important to understand. I have a two-hour podcast, okay? and it's one hour and 55 minutes long. Okay, and in that podcast, it was on this uh, this this podcast down here in Miami called the 'Fresh and Fit' podcast. My boys, Myron and Fresh. When I got on that podcast, I did two of them. The very first one I did was a really good indoctrination video for a more entry-level person. Then it helps me sell my Master Internet Marketing Program, a 5K offer that we have in my education company. It cost me $1. 07 to get somebody to watch the entire hour and 55-minute video. Pretty fucking sick. You know, a dollar and seven cents. So just to give you some perspective, like to get somebody to watch the full 10-minute video typically costs about a quarter. Sometimes, depending on the value density and how good the video is, 75 cents. We have some clients, their content is good, not great. We have some clients where the value density is just fucking extreme because they listen to what I say in videos like this. Like what I'm telling you now, and they just turn around and film new content for us. Some of them already film content like this. So they already have it laying around. Sometimes we can edit the content they've already got to just get it to the point where there's enough value density to run it. So ideally, we take a client's content that's like somewhere between 20 minutes long and two hours long and we edit the fuck out of it to be again highly dense with value, where every 45 seconds there's a hit of something that people would articulate back to you and say that was profound. Like, I got a lot out of that. You know, there was a tremendous amount of stuff you talked to me about. Now, here's the thing. Not all of you are great at that. Some of you just honestly kind of fucking suck with value density and making highly valuable pieces of content. You don't think that you've got enough to talk about. About, but you've got to get great at it. From what we've seen, and I just want to make sure this is clear, in highly sophisticated markets, this is like an absolute fucking must. To get good at being on camera, you've got to get good at dropping bangers like it's just a mandatory thing that helps the selling mechanism nowadays. Otherwise, your sales people are going to suffer from like low show rates, people showing up and treating it like an education call instead of a sales call, long drawn out sales processes as a result of that. You know, versus when you master this long form stuff. Dude, it helps so much. Use my YouTube as a great example of that. And just picture all the little fillers that I drop occasionally in the YouTube, like the call to actions to join stuff, the intros, the outros. Just imagine it without all of that. That's essentially what I'm talking to you about. You should follow. And I talked about this inside of a video I did right here in this exact room where I go through my YouTube ad strategy. I should say just my YouTube content strategy in general, not my ad strategy. And in that video, I just talk about this concept of the 90-10 system, where I'm trying to withhold like 90% of the valuable stuff and still put people on tremendous amounts of gain. But here's the thing— like I'm kind of an anomaly and I don't say that pretentiously. I have a absolute fuckload of experience— like I have a decade of experience doing what I do. And because I spend so much money every month, and because I also have so many people that operate at a high level in our education company that we also help, I get exposed to your problems. and I solve those problems rapidly and I like my problem to solution iteration cycle is so fast that I get exposed to so many things that I can consistently talk about, like I'm tapped in. You know, like I really do this shit. Some of you guys like, you're not in a position where you're truly like in touch with what the problems are that your current market you're selling to faces and that's why your content sucks. That's the biggest gap that I can tell you would help with increasing the value density of what you do. You've just got to get great at being tapped in. Your existing customer demographic that you want to sell to and figuring out, like, what the fuck do these people want to know? What is it that holds them back? What's going to give them that perspective? And this is the biggest thing. The perspective is the, this is my guy. Yes, there's other options out there that I could buy from. Yes, there's other companies that I could buy from. But this guy right here, he's got so much great shit in his content alone. That I don't care that there's other people out there. I'm going to buy from this guy right here, right now. I feel comfortable enough. I feel safe. That's the perspective you're trying to solidify in the mind of your potential buyers with the long-form content. So again, these could be podcasts that you've done, but you've got to cut out the bullshit. Okay, you've got to cut out all the stuff that people won't give a fuck about at all. And you've got to be critical of that. There is no... Version of you that should be like coddling yourself and they're like, 'Oh, that was kind of good, like all the kind of good shit you fucking snip it out, okay. That should not be in there. It should only be the bangers, banger after banger after banger, about 45 seconds long each. Now here's the thing, in terms of quantity. So the quantity is a little less, comparatively to the short form. The short form, we have upwards of 30 to 50 pieces of content and they hammer them for short form. Now, if you have that amount of content, long form that fits this criteria, the more the merrier, my friend. Add as much as you would like. Don't feel like there's a ceiling if you have it. Some of you, though, you're going to have to make it, and you're going to wonder, like, what's the minimum threshold I can get away with here, Jeremy? I don't have a lot of this shit that you're talking about, and I've got to go film it. How much time and effort? That's gonna be involved in this. So when we have clients, and we've only had two clients like this out of the four that I mentioned that we initially tested this on, and they just didn't have a lot. Like they had some. I would say confidently they had between like 6 and 12 pieces of these content. And we just found that wasn't enough. The threshold is ideally somewhere between 15 and 30. These long-form videos that you can pepper into this hammer them strategy—okay, and it's the same. concept as the short form hammer them sequence in terms of the tactics of what we're going to do it is to be clear, just in a separate campaign, so we have a campaign that we set up and that campaign. is typically going to be optimized for engagement. okay. And then the ad sets you have, let's use the example of 15 different ad sets. one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15. so in these 15 different ad sets. Okay, the audience is the same in each one of them. The audience, whatever that specific remarketing audience is that just took that key action that you're trying to hammer. So, for example, if they just booked a call, that's who audience would be. If they are going through your sales cycle, and your sales cycles like three weeks long, that's who this audience would be. If they just opted into a webinar, and the webinar is still whatever amount of time away, that's who this audience would be. Okay. So, the audience is that key remarketing audience. That needs hammered. Okay. Now it's important to note, inside of each of these ad sets, it's very simple: you have that one audience. As I mentioned earlier in this video, I talked about this idea of sometimes you might not have the minimum of 100 people within the audience, so therefore your ads won't deliver. You have to have 100 people minimum. Okay. So the 100 person minimum has to be met. You can beef up your audience a little bit. With whatever mechanism you'd like, whatever additional audience you want to pepper in there. Just start with the highest quality people and kind of work your way back. I also, just to be clear, I talk about this a lot inside. The hammer, them, sop, where you'd likely find this video unless you found it in the training library, if you have not read through the hammer, them, sop, make sure you read through the hammer, them, sop, because I talk much more in depth about it in the actual sop where I specifically say you're going to exclude people who have already bought, and you're going to make sure that you exclude people who watch three seconds of whatever is in that particular ad set, so each ad set contains one video. This is very important. If I have the campaign with, let's just say, 15 different pieces of content I want to use, that would mean I have 15 ad sets. The same in every single one of them, it's the exclusion that's different. Okay, so my audience is going to include, let's say, people who just booked a call for the last 72 hours. Okay, and that's who I'm targeting. And then, maybe I also, because I didn't have my hundred person minimum, maybe I also added an audience of people who are within my sales cycle, who just had a sales call within the last three weeks. Okay. Boom, those are my audiences. Then the only thing that's different in any one of these ad sets is the exclusion. I'm going to exclude the person who watched three seconds of whatever videos within that ad set. Okay. Now, although this is a long form piece of content, there's still a very important best practice that I want to make extremely clear. You have to make sure. That you exclude the person who watches three seconds of the video. Otherwise, that video will just consistently get back in front of that person again and again and again and again, and they won't see anything else. That's not the effect we want of the hammer. To hammer them even if they didn't consume any fucking content gives a really powerful familiarity bias at a minimum. If they don't consume any of the content, which is extremely rare. But still, it breeds familiarity bias and it makes them have this like safety perspective as a result of just seeing the quantity of content and the breadth— the breadth of content that you've got, okay? It's a very wide range of things that they're gonna see within this key timeframe that you're hammering them. So again, as soon as they watch three seconds, whether they watched fucking 10 seconds of it, whether they watched 50% of it, we don't give a fuck about that. We just want them to see that piece of content, ideally watch it. Yes, that is absolutely what we want. But again, as soon as they watch three seconds, we don't want them to see it again. We want them to see one of the other 15 pieces of content in this example. You see what I'm saying? That's the impression we're trying to give to the lead who's going through this. Otherwise, I just want to be clear again. That person. Only gonna see that single piece of content again and again and again and again and it's not gonna show them anything else. Okay, that's why we do the three-second exclusion. The other thing that I want to make clear is these audiences are dynamically updating, okay? So make sure you read through the Hammer Them SOP for that. Because in the Hammer Them SOP, I talk in depth about the fact that you want to make sure the audiences are dynamic. They should not be static. They have to be updating in real time. Meaning, when somebody books a call, they're added into the audience after that threshold passes. Let's say, for example, 72 hours or weeks, whatever you want it to be. They drop out of the audience automatically. You could do that really easily through almost every CRM that exists. With the marketing automation, you could set up. You could also do that pretty easily. Zapier, as an example, just in case your CRM does not have that functionality. Where all you're doing, just to be clear, is adding the person in the audience, defining a time frame for how long you wait, and then it just removes the person from the audience. That's as simple as the marketing automation is to do that. Dynamic updating. It's not more complicated than that, okay? So anyway. Inside of each ad set is just the one video. Okay? Now, I want to be clear. You have a choice, by the way, here. You can really do it either way. You can do CBO, which is campaign budget optimization, or you can do ABO, which is ad set budget optimization. Campaign budget optimization will technically bias the spend. Towards certain ad sets in this case, particular videos that are outperforming the others from a statistics perspective of engagement, however, I generally will use ad set budget optimization because I want to force the spend on each one of these ad sets independently. I don't want the campaign to bias towards particular videos and ignore others. Although, to be fair, that does technically accomplish what we're looking for either way. It's not like it complicates it. It's really just an option. I generally still run ad set budget optimization, though, just to disclose. I test campaign budget optimization just to see what it does. And because I still have the three-second exclusions, I generally... Really still see every one of the however many videos I put in there seeing distribution but when I force ad set budget optimization onto it I see not necessarily equal distribution, but a lot more equal distribution compared to campaign budget optimization. So I prefer ad set budget optimization (ABO) just to be clear. The budgets on these are about the same. Same as the Hydra campaign for the short form, we're generally running like $5 a day. And if we don't see the frequency count that we want on the campaign level for the total quantity of frequency in the desired timeframe, exceeding about 15 to 20. or whatever your threshold is. Like if you have 15 long form pieces of content. Your desired frequency might be half of that. You might have, for example, a frequency kind of like five to ten. That you ideally want to see, with whatever the time frame is that you defined. So, if it's 72 hours before their call, ideally they see, for example, five to ten different pieces of long-form content before that call occurs. If you're remarketing to somebody over an entire duration of, say, three weeks, as an example, just going through your sales process, ideally the frequency count throughout that entire three weeks would be whatever the total quantity of videos is that you have in there, or it would exceed that frequency would be even better. That's the target, right? The shorter the timeframe. Technically, you still want to have a very high frequency count, but the longer the time frame that you're doing the remarketing, the more you can be a little more aggressive in the sense that you want to see the frequency higher right. So anyway, very simple campaign to set up. It is separate from the short form campaign. We do want to separate them. Because if you combine them, the unfortunate caveat of that is that the campaign will always... Bias towards the short form it will not distribute the long form equally proportionate to the short form technically to be fair you could set up your short form and your long form alongside of each other and use ad set budget optimization. I totally understand that. I'm not oblivious to that. However, I like to separate out the analytics. I like to have one campaign that's for sure. Form and one campaign that's for long form. That way I can ensure because my frequency count is going to be different. Like I want a much higher frequency count in my short form because there's much more short form content. I technically still want a high frequency count in my long form, but it's not as high as the short form. That's why I like to separate it. It's really just from an analytic perspective, so it makes it easier so I can monitor. Things, adjust budgets up and down accordingly, etc. A lot of you ask, what should the daily budget be? Typically, you're going to start with somewhere between like $5 and $10 a day on an ad set budget. Per video, depending on the audience size. I don't give a fuck about whatever the cost per whatever it is that I'm optimizing for. Ideally, by the way, just to be clear in terms of how you're optimizing on. Ad set level on the campaign you've selected, engagement on the ad set level, you ideally want to select video views and optimize for through play, which is a 15-second video view threshold. People who are more probable to watch the videos for longer durations. Um, okay. All this information is really, really effective. The long form creates a tremendous impact on the sales process, even if you don't have the salespeople adapt their tactics and strategies. I'm a big fan of this. Not necessarily upset, you know, that I didn't incorporate long form initially into the hammer them, because the short form, to be fair, still does a tremendous job. This is just intended to amplify the results that we generate. That much more, that's really all it comes down to is we're just trying to increase conversion rates, increase show rates, condense sales cycles, and increase AOVs to that more effective of a level you know. It's really all comes down to this: it's just a giant propaganda machine. This is just content marketing 101. If you have any follow-up questions, feel free to DM me. I'm happy to answer those questions or ask a question in the group chat as well if you'd like. All right. Thank you. See you in the next video.
